questions.text: Which of following compound is solid at room temperature?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethanol is a liquid at room temperature due to its low molecular weight and hydrogen bonding.
Phenol is a solid at room temperature (melting point ~40-42°C). It exists as colorless crystalline solid due to stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
Butane is a gas at room temperature (boiling point ~ -1°C).
﻿ Methanol is a liquid at room temperature, with a boiling point of ~65°C.

questions.explanation: Phenol is a solid at room temperature (melting point ~40-42°C). It exists as colorless crystalline solid due to stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

questions.text: Introduction of RCO group to a compound is called
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is not a standard term. Possibly confused with aldehyde formation, but not applicable to RCO group.
No such widely recognized reaction term.
Acylation is the process of adding an acyl group (RCO–) to a compound, commonly seen in Friedel-Crafts acylation.
It refers to addition of hydrogen (H₂), not RCO group.

questions.explanation: Acylation is the process of adding an acyl group (RCO–) to a compound, commonly seen in Friedel-Crafts acylation.

questions.text: What happens when naphthalene balls are put inside kerosene:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Naphthalene doesn’t precipitate; it’s already in solid form.
Naphthalene has low solubility at room temperature in kerosene
Naphthalene is soluble in nonpolar solvents like kerosene, but it needs to be heated to dissolve well
It can dissolve when heated.

questions.explanation: Naphthalene is soluble in nonpolar solvents like kerosene, but it needs to be heated to dissolve well

questions.text: Three fused benzene rings are found in:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Naphthalene has only two fused benzene rings
It is a complex indicator compound but not a fused-ring system.
It has three benzene rings, but they are not fused; they are connected to a central carbon
Anthracene consists of three fused benzene rings in a straight line.

questions.explanation: Anthracene consists of three fused benzene rings in a straight line.

questions.text: After ozonolysis of benzene, the product is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is an intermediate formed in the ozonolysis of benzene.
Ozonolysis ultimately breaks the ring to give glyoxal (ethanedial).
Same as above
All are correct stages/products during ozonolysis.

questions.explanation: All are correct stages/products during ozonolysis.

questions.text: Catalytic dehydrogenation of n-heptane in presence of Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃ at 750 K gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is an isomer, not a dehydrogenation product
Dehydrocyclization of n-heptane under these conditions forms toluene (methylbenzene).
This could result from simple dehydrogenation, but not under these aromatic-forming conditions.
Not expected in this catalytic process.

questions.explanation: Dehydrocyclization of n-heptane under these conditions forms toluene (methylbenzene).

questions.text: Bromine in an inert solvent is added separately to hexane, hexene, benzene and methylbenzene. In which of the following pairs will the observations be the same?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Reacts with Br₂ by addition (decolorizes bromine water).
Substitutes Br under light or catalyst.
Hexane shows No reaction with Br₂
Correct as both decolorize bromine (one by addition, one by substitution).

questions.explanation: Correct as both decolorize bromine (one by addition, one by substitution).

questions.text: Heterolytic cleavage may lead to _ attacks.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Partly correct. Negative ion (nucleophile) is formed.
Partly correct. Positive ion (electrophile) is formed
Both nucleophiles and electrophiles can arise from heterolytic cleavage.
It’s a type of coal, unrelated to organic mechanisms.

questions.explanation: Both nucleophiles and electrophiles can arise from heterolytic cleavage.

questions.text: Due to the presence of an unpaired electron, free radicals are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Free radicals are highly reactive due to unpaired electron.
Opposite of the correct property.
Free radicals are neutral, not charged.
Not necessarily positively charged

questions.explanation: Free radicals are highly reactive due to unpaired electron.

questions.text: The reaction between electrophile and nucleophile involves
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Not a full electron transfer.
Electrophile accepts electrons; nucleophile donates a pair to form a covalent bond.
Though similar, the general term here is covalent.
A covalent bond is formed.

questions.explanation: Electrophile accepts electrons; nucleophile donates a pair to form a covalent bond.

questions.text: Electrophilic reagents are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: These are nucleophiles or Lewis bases
Electrophiles accept electron pairs, hence they are Lewis acids.
These are free radicals, not necessarily electrophiles
These donate electron pairs, so they are nucleophiles.

questions.explanation: Electrophiles accept electron pairs, hence they are Lewis acids.

questions.text: Which compound is inorganic in nature but shows properties like organic compounds?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Although historically debated, urea is considered organic now
A simple inorganic ion, doesn’t show typical organic properties.
Inorganic by classification, but non-polar, covalent, behaves like organic solvents.
It’s a salt, formed from an acid and base, not similar to organic compounds in behavior.

questions.explanation: Inorganic by classification, but non-polar, covalent, behaves like organic solvents.

questions.text: Ethyl isopropyl ether and ethyl n-propyl ether is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Chain isomerism refers to different carbon chain arrangements.
Metamers are same functional group but different alkyl groups on either side of the ether oxygen.
Functional group doesn’t change (still ethers).
No change in functional group position, just in side groups

questions.explanation: Metamers are same functional group but different alkyl groups on either side of the ether oxygen.

questions.text: Which are isomers?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Not same molecular formula.
Different molecular formula.
Formula is same (C₃H₆O), but correct answer is still B due to matching organic types.
They are functional group isomers (carboxylic acid vs ester), same formula (C₃H₆O₂)

questions.explanation: They are functional group isomers (carboxylic acid vs ester), same formula (C₃H₆O₂)

questions.text: The free radical mechanism is always associated with _ mechanism.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Free radicals are formed when bonds break evenly—homolytic cleavage.
Ionic reactions involve charged species, not radicals
Produces ions, not radicals
Elimination may not always involve radicals.

questions.explanation: Free radicals are formed when bonds break evenly—homolytic cleavage.

questions.text: 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane are isomers of each other. Such type of isomerism is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Applies to alkenes, not saturated haloalkanes.
Carbon chain is same
The position of the Cl atom differs on the same carbon skeleton.
Functional group remains the same (halogen).

questions.explanation: The position of the Cl atom differs on the same carbon skeleton.

questions.text: Nucleophiles are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nucleophiles donate electron pairs, hence are Lewis bases.
Electrophiles, not nucleophiles.
Most nucleophiles are negative or neutral with lone pairs.
Not all radicals act as nucleophiles.

questions.explanation: Nucleophiles donate electron pairs, hence are Lewis bases.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a powerful electrophile used to attack electrons of benzene ring?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: It’s a catalyst that helps generate Cl⁺
Similar to FeCl₃ but for bromination.
The actual electrophile that attacks the benzene ring in halogenation
A nucleophile, not electrophile.

questions.explanation: The actual electrophile that attacks the benzene ring in halogenation

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs can be cis-trans isomer to each other?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CH₂=CH₂ cannot have cis-trans isomerism (no different groups)
Not possible due to identical groups on one end
But-2-ene can exist as cis and trans isomers.
One is alkene, other is alkane.

questions.explanation: But-2-ene can exist as cis and trans isomers.

questions.text: The compound which is not isomeric with diethyl ether is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Same formula (C₄H₁₀O), different structure.
﻿Isomeric. Alcohol form with same formula.
Has same formula but straight chain structure that does not fit as an isomer with ether group in diethyl ether.
Isomeric. Even though small, has similar elements in functional form.

questions.explanation: Has same formula but straight chain structure that does not fit as an isomer with ether group in diethyl ether.

questions.text: The phenomenon of metamerism is shown by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Methyl n-propyl ether and diethyl ether
options.explanation: Metamers have same functional group but different alkyl groups on both sides of the group (here, ethers).
These are functional group isomers, not metamers.
These are functional group isomers, not metamers
These are homologs (differ by -CH₂), not metamers.

questions.explanation: Metamers have same functional group but different alkyl groups on both sides of the group (here, ethers).

questions.text: Which one of the following conditions is required for thermal cracking:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Thermal cracking occurs at high temperature (~700°C).
These catalysts aid the process.
Aeration prevents formation of soot.
All are essential for efficient cracking.

questions.explanation: All are essential for efficient cracking.

questions.text: In cis-trans isomerism, the trans isomer has all of the following properties EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Trans-isomers are usually non-polar
Trans-isomers are symmetrical.
Dipole moments cancel out, so μ = 0
Trans-isomers have higher melting points due to symmetry and better packing in crystals.

questions.explanation: Trans-isomers have higher melting points due to symmetry and better packing in crystals.

questions.text: Which of the following is not nucleophile:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Lone pairs on O
Nucleophile. Lone pair on N.
Electrophile, acts as Lewis acid
Nucleophile as Negative charge = strong nucleophile

questions.explanation: Electrophile, acts as Lewis acid

questions.text: Which of the following compounds is not a phenol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenol. Benzene ring with -OH.
Phenol. Dihydroxybenzene (resorcinol, catechol, etc.).
This is tert-butanol, an alcohol, not a phenol.
Another dihydroxy derivative.

questions.explanation: This is tert-butanol, an alcohol, not a phenol.

questions.text: Compound with the highest hydrogen to carbon ratio is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Less hydrogen than alkanes due to double bond
Cyclization removes 2 H atoms.
Aromatic structure = even fewer Hs
Alkanes have general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ = max H/C ratio.

questions.explanation: Alkanes have general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ = max H/C ratio.

questions.text: The reaction CH₃CH₂Cl + LiAlH₄ → CH₃CH₃ + LiCl + AlH₃ is an example of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: No double/triple bond formed.
No atoms added across a bond.
Cl is replaced by H → nucleophilic substitution.
No structural rearrangement occurs.

questions.explanation: Cl is replaced by H → nucleophilic substitution.

questions.text: The temperature used for the hydrogenation of alkenes using Ni is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes using Ni occurs around 200°C.
Too high for safe hydrogenation; may cause other reactions or decomposition.
Too high for safe hydrogenation; may cause other reactions or decomposition.
Too high for safe hydrogenation; may cause other reactions or decomposition.

questions.explanation: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes using Ni occurs around 200°C.

questions.text: Hydrogenation of unsaturated oils is done by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Catalyst for hydrogenation in margarine production.
Not used for this purpose.
Catalyst in sulphuric acid manufacturing.
Copper used in dehydrogenation, not hydrogenation

questions.explanation: Catalyst for hydrogenation in margarine production.

questions.text: To prepare alkanes from alkyl halides, the process used is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Halogen is substituted by hydrogen via reduction (e.g., with LiAlH₄ or Zn/HCl)
Incorrect as this reaction Forms alkenes.
Incorrect as this process Produces alcohols, not alkanes.
Incorrect as Substitution is valid.

questions.explanation: Halogen is substituted by hydrogen via reduction (e.g., with LiAlH₄ or Zn/HCl)

questions.text: The chemical name of the insecticide Gammexane is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: DDT is a different insecticide (dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane).
It’s a synonym, but not the proper chemical name.
It’s the IUPAC name for Gammexane
It's an unrelated compound with only 2 carbon atoms.

questions.explanation: It’s the IUPAC name for Gammexane

questions.text: Gammexane is obtained from benzene when it reacts with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Cl₂ in bright sunlight (absence of catalyst)
options.explanation: In sunlight, benzene reacts with Cl₂ by free radical addition, forming Gammexane.

options.text: Cl₂ in bright sunlight (presence of catalyst)
options.explanation: Catalyst not required here; catalyst would lead to substitution, not addition.

options.text: CH₃Cl in the presence of anhydrous AlCl₃
options.explanation: These are Friedel-Crafts alkylation conditions.
These are Friedel-Crafts alkylation conditions.

questions.explanation: In sunlight, benzene reacts with Cl₂ by free radical addition, forming Gammexane.

questions.text: Point out the wrong statement about benzene’s structure:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It forms only one monosubstituted product
options.explanation: Due to symmetry, all positions are equivalent.

options.text: All C–C bond distances are uniformly 1.397 Å
options.explanation: Confirmed by X-ray diffraction studies.

options.text: It is a resonance hybrid of several canonical forms
options.explanation: Benzene is a resonance hybrid

options.text: It has three delocalized π-molecular orbitals
options.explanation: It has one delocalized π-system, not three separate π-orbitals.

questions.explanation: It has one delocalized π-system, not three separate π-orbitals.

questions.text: Anti-Markovnikov’s product is given by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Propene is capable, but the more distinct case is 1-butene.
1-Butene gives anti-Markovnikov product due to unsymmetrical double bond
2-Butene is symmetrical → no anti-Markovnikov orientation.
Only B is correct.

questions.explanation: 1-Butene gives anti-Markovnikov product due to unsymmetrical double bond

questions.text: Which of the following sets cannot be used for alcohol dehydration?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CaCl₂ is a drying agent, not a dehydrating catalyst.
These promote dehydration of alcohols to alkenes.
These promote dehydration of alcohols to alkenes.
These promote dehydration of alcohols to alkenes.

questions.explanation: CaCl₂ is a drying agent, not a dehydrating catalyst.

questions.text: Anti-Markovnikov's addition is observed in
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: No difference in where addition occurs.
Anti-Markovnikov addition happens with unsymmetrical alkenes in presence of peroxide.
Reaction is not with alkyl halides
Reaction is not with alkyl halides

questions.explanation: Anti-Markovnikov addition happens with unsymmetrical alkenes in presence of peroxide.

questions.text: Alkenes usually undergo:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Double bond is electron-rich → attracts electrophiles.
Not favorable due to repulsion.
This is common in aromatic compounds.
Alkenes don't usually undergo substitution

questions.explanation: Double bond is electron-rich → attracts electrophiles.

questions.text: Nitration of toluene with H₂SO₄ at 60°C gives predominantly:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ortho product is formed but not major due to steric hindrance.
Methyl group is an activating, ortho/para-directing group.
This is a disubstituted product, not predominant at 60°C
Para product dominates due to less steric hindrance

questions.explanation: Para product dominates due to less steric hindrance

questions.text: When 2-bromobutane reacts with alcoholic KOH, the major product is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alcohol, not alkene.
Less substituted alkene → not major
According to Saytzeff’s Rule, more substituted alkene is major product.
Ether formation, irrelevant here.

questions.explanation: According to Saytzeff’s Rule, more substituted alkene is major product.

questions.text: Hydrogenation of benzene with Ni at 200°C gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Full hydrogenation of benzene forms cyclohexane.
These are intermediates or incorrect names (cyclohexatriene = benzene itself).
These are intermediates or incorrect names (cyclohexatriene = benzene itself).
These are intermediates or incorrect names (cyclohexatriene = benzene itself).

questions.explanation: Full hydrogenation of benzene forms cyclohexane.
